name: Awakening: Sense
text:
It happens at the market. Between one breath and the next, as if someone opened a door you didn't know was there.

The baker's hands are shaking. You've bought bread from him every week for two years and you've never noticed his hands shake, but now you can't stop noticing. His left hand trembles when he reaches for the loaf. His smile doesn't match his eyes. Something is wrong in his life, something heavy, and it's written all over him in a language you couldn't read until ten seconds ago.

The woman at the next stall is afraid of the man she's standing with. The child near the fountain is pretending to cry. The old man on the bench is listening to a conversation three stalls over and trying to look like he isn't.

You know all of this. You know it the way you know the sun is out — not because someone told you, but because you can feel it on your skin.

Then the baker asks if you want the usual and his voice has texture in it you've never heard before. Layers. A tiredness underneath the politeness, and underneath the tiredness something worse, and you need to stop listening because if you hear one more layer you're going to cry in the middle of the market for a man whose name you don't even know.

You drop the bread. You leave. You sit behind the granary with your head between your knees and breathe until the world gets smaller again.

It takes days. The world doesn't get smaller. You just learn which parts to listen to.
